summary: Angel Reese comes to Atlanta in a blockbuster Dream trade, the BeltLine seeks a builder for its trickiest trail segment through south Buckhead, and MARTA opens all faregates during the better Breeze transition. Plus: Koshu Club brings Showa-era Japanese dining to Buckhead, and the Braves brawl with the Angels after a heated mound charge.

topStory.headline: Angel Reese Traded to the Atlanta Dream for Two First-Round Picks
topStory.summary: The WNBA All-Star forward heads to Atlanta from the Chicago Sky in exchange for the Dream's 2027 and 2028 first-round picks. Reese averaged 14.7 points and 12 rebounds last season.
topStory.body:
One of the WNBA's most popular players is heading to Atlanta. Angel Reese, the former LSU star and two-time All-Star forward, was traded to the Atlanta Dream on April 6 in exchange for two first-round picks (2027 and 2028).

Reese's numbers tell the story of an elite rebounder: 14.7 points, 12 rebounds, 3.7 assists, and 1.5 steals per game last season with the Chicago Sky. Her 5.1 offensive rebounds per game led the league.

The Dream's front court gets significantly more physical with the 6-3 Reese joining Brittney Griner, Rhyne Howard, and All-League selection Allisha Gray. The roster now has serious depth across every position.

The Dream open the preseason on May 3 against the Washington Mystics at Gateway Center Arena in College Park. The regular season tips off May 17 when the reigning champion Las Vegas Aces visit State Farm Arena.

For a franchise that has built steadily through the draft and free agency, adding a player of Reese's caliber signals the Dream are ready to compete for a championship. The trade gives Atlanta one of the most talented rosters in the Eastern Conference heading into a season with rising expectations.
topStory.imageAlt: Angel Reese, newly traded to the Atlanta Dream

stories.headline: BeltLine Seeks Builder for Trickiest Trail Segment Through South Buckhead
stories.summary: Atlanta Beltline Inc. has released a bid for 2.8 miles of Northeast Trail through the Armour/Ottley district, including four connector trails linking to MARTA's Lindbergh station and PATH400.
stories.body:
The most complex section of the BeltLine trail is officially moving toward construction. Atlanta Beltline Inc. released an Invitation to Bid seeking firms to build Northeast Trail Segment 3 and four connector trails across south Buckhead.

The federally funded project covers 2.8 total miles of trails through the Armour/Ottley district and Lindbergh/Uptown areas. For context, that's roughly the same distance as the BeltLine stretch between Piedmont Park and Breaker Breaker restaurant.

The connector trails will provide critical links from the main BeltLine loop to MARTA's Lindbergh station, Buckhead's PATH400 trail system, the Armour-Ottley commercial district (home to SweetWater Brewing Company and Painted Pickle), and local streets.

The project marks the final major segment of the Northeast Trail to enter construction, closing a gap over Peachtree Creek and active CSX and Norfolk Southern railroad corridors. The railroad crossings are what make this section especially challenging from an engineering standpoint.

With the Eastside and Westside trails largely complete, this segment represents one of the last pieces needed to close the 22-mile loop that has reshaped Atlanta's transportation and development landscape.
stories.imageAlt: Rendering of BeltLine Northeast Trail Segment 3 through south Buckhead

stories.headline: Atlanta Ranked Among Top 5 Cities for First-Time Homebuyers
stories.summary: Zillow's 2026 analysis of the 50 largest U.S. metro areas places Atlanta in the top five for first-time buyers, citing rent affordability, housing supply, and a growing job market.
stories.body:
Atlanta continues to attract first-time homebuyers. Zillow's latest analysis of the 50 largest U.S. metro areas ranks the city among the top five markets for people buying their first home in 2026.

The ranking weighs four key factors: rent affordability (lower rents make it easier to save for a down payment), home affordability (homes available within a typical buyer's budget), housing supply (more inventory means more options and less competition), and market forecast (whether home values are expected to grow or decline).

Atlanta scored well across multiple categories, with its growing job market and expanding housing inventory cited as advantages. The city's relative affordability compared to coastal metros continues to draw transplants from more expensive markets.

For a metro area that has seen rapid price appreciation over the past five years, the ranking suggests Atlanta still offers a realistic path to homeownership for buyers who have been priced out of other major cities.
stories.imageAlt: Atlanta skyline at dawn, ranked among top cities for first-time homebuyers

stories.headline: MARTA Opens All Faregates During Better Breeze Transition
stories.summary: All MARTA faregates will remain open until May 2 as the agency rolls out its new tap-to-pay system. Riders must switch to a new orange Breeze card or use contactless bank cards by the deadline.
stories.body:
For the next few weeks, all of MARTA's faregates will be open. But that doesn't mean free rides.

MARTA officials say faregates will stay open until May 2 to help customers get acquainted with the upgraded better Breeze fare payment system. The open gates ensure nobody is denied access because of transition hiccups as new faregates and ticket vending machines are installed across the system.

Riders can buy new fares from new ticket vending machines (where available), Ride Stores, or breezecard.com. Alternatively, riders can use a bank card or mobile wallet to pay at new faregates and bus validators where they're active.

The critical detail: old fare products are no longer being sold, cannot be loaded, and do not work on the new system. All customers must switch to a new orange Breeze card or use open payment (contactless bank card or phone) by May 2.

The transition marks MARTA's biggest technology upgrade in years, moving from the aging Breeze card system to a modern tap-to-pay platform that accepts contactless credit cards, debit cards, and mobile wallets alongside the new Breeze cards.
stories.imageAlt: MARTA's new better Breeze fare system equipment

stories.headline: Delta Raises Checked Bag Fees as Jet Fuel Costs Climb
stories.summary: Atlanta-based Delta Air Lines joins a growing list of U.S. carriers hiking checked bag fees as higher fuel costs ripple through the airline industry.
stories.body:
Atlanta-based Delta Air Lines is raising its checked bag fees, joining a wave of U.S. carriers responding to soaring jet fuel prices.

The increase comes as fuel costs ripple through the airline industry, squeezing margins on both domestic and international routes. For Atlanta travelers who depend on Hartsfield-Jackson as a Delta hub, the higher fees add to the cost of flying at a time when gas prices are also climbing above $4 a gallon nationally.

Delta's announcement follows similar moves by other major carriers. The airline industry has been grappling with elevated fuel costs tied in part to global oil market disruptions, including tensions surrounding the Strait of Hormuz shipping lane.

For the world's busiest airport by passenger traffic, any Delta pricing change affects millions of travelers passing through Atlanta each year.
stories.imageAlt: Delta Air Lines logo at Hartsfield-Jackson Atlanta International Airport

stories.headline: Michelin-Starred Mujo Team Opens Koshu Club in Buckhead April 14
stories.summary: Castellucci Hospitality Group's newest restaurant brings Showa-era Japanese dining to the 99 West Paces building. The 46-seat spot features robatayaki grilling and a fusion of Western and traditional Japanese dishes.
stories.body:
The team behind Michelin-starred Mujo is opening Koshu Club on April 14 at the 99 West Paces building in Buckhead, across from the St. Regis hotel.

The 46-seat restaurant leans into Japan's Showa-era cuisine (1926 to 1989), a period when Western-influenced cooking fused with traditional Japanese dishes in supper clubs and night market stalls across the country.

Mujo culinary director Jordan Trent Harris oversees the kitchen with Chef de Cuisine Keith Miller. The menu is built around a three-course structure with robatayaki-style grilling, plus plates integrating Italian and French techniques. Expect dishes like gyukatsu (wagyu sandwich), mushroom doria, and American wagyu filet with demi-glace.

Smith Hanes Studio designed the moody, intimate space inspired by mid-century Japanese minimalism. Music taps into Showa-era supper club energy.

Castellucci Hospitality Group also operates Iberian Pig, Cooks & Soldiers, Sugo, and Double Zero. For CEO Fred Castellucci, the restaurant fills a gap in Atlanta's Japanese dining scene beyond omakase and sushi.
stories.imageAlt: Dishes at Koshu Club including mushroom doria, yamitsuki cabbage, and American wagyu filet

stories.headline: Chirori Proves Casual Sushi Works in Midtown
stories.summary: The 14th Street spot has quietly built a following since 2019 with its approachable omakase ($99 dinner, $65 lunch) and strong a la carte nigiri. Try the hamachi with pesto.
stories.body:
Tucked along 14th Street in Midtown, Chirori has quietly built a following for its approachable omakase and solid sake selection since opening in 2019. Dinner omakase runs $99 (with a $65 lunch option), making it one of the more accessible entry points into the format in Atlanta.

Next door to sister restaurant Wagaya, a longtime ramen spot with a small Japanese grocery, the pair anchors a cozy corner for casual Japanese dining. The a la carte menu shines with individually priced nigiri, sushi rolls, seaweed salads, and grilled dishes.

Standouts include the hamachi with pesto ($7.50), where green herbs lift the fresh fish in a rare sushi pairing, and the o-toro tuna with fresh uni ($13). For an easier route, the chef's 10-piece nigiri selection ($45) highlights the kitchen's strengths.

The Suigei tokubetsu junmai sake ("Drunken Whale," $16 a glass) pairs well with everything on the menu. Ask for a wine glass instead of the traditional cup for better aromatics.
stories.imageAlt: Assorted nigiri and rainbow sushi roll at Chirori in Midtown

stories.headline: Restaurant Roundup: Brew Dog on BeltLine Closes, Agave Shuts Down
stories.summary: A wave of closings hit the metro area. Brew Dog on the BeltLine at Krog Street served its final beer after four years. Mojave in Sandy Springs also shuttered after seven years.
stories.body:
A wave of restaurant closings has hit the Atlanta area over the past few weeks, with several longtime spots shuttering with little notice.

Brew Dog on the BeltLine announced it served its final beer on March 8 after four years at its Krog Street location. The Scottish brewery's craft beer bar had been a popular stop for BeltLine walkers. "We are incredibly grateful to everyone who joined us," the bar said on Instagram.

Mojave in Sandy Springs also closed immediately on March 8 after seven years. The Latin American restaurant was known for house-made tamales and paella.

Meanwhile, Agave, the longtime Cabbagetown Mexican restaurant, announced its closure after years as a neighborhood anchor. The restaurant had been a staple on Carroll Street and a favorite among east side residents.

On the brighter side, several new concepts are headed to the metro area, including Bora Bora Smoothie Cafe at Plaza Midtown and multiple new pizza spots across the suburbs.
stories.imageAlt: Atlanta restaurant scene roundup showing recent closures and openings

stories.headline: Bill Signing Season Begins as Georgia's 2026 Legislative Session Wraps
stories.summary: Governor Kemp has until May 12 to sign or veto bills passed this session, including controversial HB 369 making certain metro Atlanta elected offices nonpartisan. The Cobb County DA has promised a legal challenge.
stories.body:
Georgia's 2026 legislative session is over, but the real action is just beginning. Governor Brian Kemp has 40 days (until May 12) to sign or veto the measures passed by state lawmakers. Any unsigned bills automatically become law after that deadline.

Among the most closely watched bills is House Bill 369, which would make certain elected positions in five metro Atlanta counties nonpartisan, including the office of district attorney. Cobb County DA Sonya Allen has promised to join a lawsuit challenging the legislation if Kemp signs it.

The session also produced education bills heading to Kemp's desk, including a high school cell phone ban, need-based scholarship expansion, and literacy funding. A bill allowing Georgians to get birth control directly from pharmacists is also awaiting the governor's signature.

Kemp acknowledged his power in his final term during the State of the State address: "For those who may think I'm a lame duck, I still have a big red pen." Last year he vetoed seven bills.
stories.imageAlt: Governor Kemp at a bill signing ceremony at the Georgia State Capitol

stories.headline: Four Fulton County Commission Seats Up for Grabs in May Primary
stories.summary: Half the Fulton County Commission is on the ballot May 19, including the chair seat where incumbent Robb Pitts faces challenges from commissioners Mo Ivory and Marvin Arrington Jr.
stories.body:
Fulton County voters will choose half their county commissioners in the May 19 primary. Districts 1, 3, and 5 are up, plus the countywide commission chair, where incumbent Robb Pitts faces Democratic primary challenges from two sitting commissioners, Mo Ivory and Marvin Arrington Jr.

The seven-member Fulton County Commission oversees county operations including elections, public health, animal control, and the library system. It also funds the courts, the DA and public defender offices, the sheriff, and the county jail.

The chair sets the agenda and presides over meetings but has the same voting power as any other commissioner. For Fulton residents wondering what they're actually voting for, former county and city attorney Patrise Perkins-Hooker explains that the commission controls services in unincorporated areas and countywide functions, while the 15 cities within Fulton (including Atlanta) handle their own local services.

The race for chair could reshape Fulton County's leadership at a critical time for the region.
stories.imageAlt: Fulton County Commission meeting ahead of the May 2026 primary election

stories.headline: Atlanta Banned Data Centers in Most of the City. One Is Trying Anyway.
stories.summary: Despite Atlanta's 2025 moratorium on data centers in most zones, at least one developer is seeking an exception, testing the city's stance on the energy-intensive facilities.
stories.body:
Atlanta banned data centers in most of the city last year, but at least one developer is pushing to build anyway.

The city's 2025 moratorium responded to concerns about data centers' massive energy consumption, noise, and limited job creation relative to their land footprint. The facilities have become a flashpoint across metro Atlanta as demand for cloud computing and data storage has exploded.

The developer seeking an exception is testing whether Atlanta's policy will hold firm or bend under pressure from an industry pouring billions into Georgia. The state's cheap electricity and fiber-optic infrastructure have made it a magnet for data center investment, with massive facilities already operating in Douglas County, Newton County, and other exurban locations.

For Atlanta neighborhoods, the debate centers on whether the economic benefits of data centers justify their impact on the power grid and surrounding communities. The Georgia General Assembly also considered data center legislation this session, though those bills did not advance.
stories.imageAlt: Atlanta data center moratorium challenged by developer

stories.headline: Braves, Angels Benches Clear in Wild Brawl After Soler Charges Mound
stories.summary: Former teammates Jorge Soler and Reynaldo Lopez came to blows in the fifth inning. Braves manager Walt Weiss tackled Soler to the ground. Both players were ejected; Atlanta won 7-2.
stories.body:
The Atlanta Braves' Tuesday night game against the Los Angeles Angels turned into a bench-clearing brawl after Angels slugger Jorge Soler charged the mound against Braves pitcher Reynaldo Lopez.

The incident erupted in the bottom of the fifth inning when a Lopez pitch went high and inside, tipping off catcher Jonah Heim's glove. Soler took exception and charged at Lopez with haymakers flying. Lopez threw punches to defend himself before the benches emptied.

Braves manager Walt Weiss, who has a background in martial arts and MMA training, tackled Soler to the ground. "That's a big man, so I felt I've gotta get him off his feet because he's gonna hurt somebody," Weiss said.

The two players were teammates on the 2024 Braves. Soler was the World Series MVP when Atlanta won it all in 2021.

"I asked him if everything was OK and the answer he gave me, I didn't like it," Soler said through a translator. Lopez maintained there was no intent to hit anyone. Both players were ejected. The Braves won the game 7-2.
stories.imageAlt: Braves pitcher Reynaldo Lopez and Angels slugger Jorge Soler during the bench-clearing brawl

stories.headline: Hawks Add Tony Bradley for Playoff Push
stories.summary: Atlanta signed former Pacers center Tony Bradley to bolster inside depth as the team makes its late-season push toward the postseason.
stories.body:
The Atlanta Hawks are bolstering their frontcourt for the stretch run. The team signed former Indiana Pacers center Tony Bradley to provide additional inside depth as they push toward the playoffs.

Bradley, a veteran big man, gives the Hawks another option in the paint alongside the team's existing rotation. His size and physicality address a need that became apparent during a recent loss to the New York Knicks, a potential first-round playoff matchup.

The Hawks have been in and out of the playoff picture all season, and the Bradley signing signals the front office believes this roster has a real shot at postseason basketball. The move comes as Atlanta enters the final stretch of the regular season with several games remaining against Eastern Conference opponents jockeying for seeding.
stories.imageAlt: Atlanta Hawks sign center Tony Bradley for playoff push

stories.headline: Gordon Parks' Rare Color Photos of the Segregated South on View in Atlanta
stories.summary: Jackson Fine Art in Buckhead is showing 'Gordon Parks: The South in Color,' featuring rare color transparencies from the photographer's landmark 1956 Life magazine assignment documenting segregation in Alabama.
stories.body:
More than 70 color transparencies from photographer Gordon Parks' landmark 1956 trip through the segregated South are on display at Jackson Fine Art in Buckhead.

'Gordon Parks: The South in Color' features images from the assignment that became his epochal Life magazine photo essay documenting the daily reality of segregation in Mobile, Alabama, centering on the Thornton family.

Gallery owner Anna Walker Skillman has a long history with Parks' work. When the color transparencies were discovered in an old storage bin in 2011, the Gordon Parks Foundation contacted Skillman for their first exhibition in 2012. She later dovetailed the celebrated 'Gordon Parks: Segregation Story' exhibition at the High Museum in 2015.

The most iconic image from the series shows Joanne Thornton Wilson and her niece Shirley Anne Kirksey standing beneath a neon 'colored entrance' sign in Mobile. Over a decade later, the work remains urgent and relevant.
stories.imageAlt: Gordon Parks color photograph from 1956 depicting segregation in the American South

stories.headline: Black Effect Podcast Festival Returns to Pullman Yards April 25
stories.summary: Charlamagne Tha God, DJ Envy, and the iHeartMedia Black Effect Podcast Network bring live tapings of 'Drink Champs' and more to East Atlanta's Pullman Yards for the festival's fourth year.
stories.body:
The Black Effect Podcast Festival is returning to Atlanta on April 25, bringing live tapings, panel discussions, and audience engagement to Pullman Yards in Kirkwood.

Now in its fourth year, the festival will be co-hosted by Charlamagne Tha God and DJ Envy of 'The Breakfast Club,' the nationally syndicated radio show that recently expanded to Netflix and has seen a surge in ratings among listeners ages 18 to 54.

Live tapings of 'Drink Champs,' 'Keep It Positive Sweetie,' 'Reality with the King,' and 'Don't Call Me White Girl' are planned. Panel discussions will cover topics including the influence of Gen Z in media and audio and media development.

The Black Effect Podcast Network, a joint venture between Charlamagne Tha God and iHeartMedia launched in 2020, has developed more than 60 shows. The festival runs from noon to 7 p.m.
stories.imageAlt: Black Effect Podcast Festival promotional image for the Pullman Yards event

stories.headline: Piedmont Park Vietnam War Memorial Plaza and Sculpture Now Complete
stories.summary: Atlanta's first proper tribute to local Vietnam War veterans is finished near the park's Active Oval. The Sons of Atlanta memorial honors 240 residents of Atlanta and Fulton County killed in action.
stories.body:
The latest addition to Piedmont Park isn't a trail or grand entry. It's a memorial that local military veterans say the city has long needed.

Construction has wrapped on the Sons of Atlanta Vietnam War Monument and Memorial Experience near the park's northernmost Piedmont Avenue entrance, just west of the Active Oval. The installation includes a tribute sculpture, plaza, and commemorative wall.

The memorial recognizes 240 residents of Atlanta and Fulton County who were killed in action during the Vietnam War. While those veterans' names appear at the Georgia State Memorial Park near the Capitol and on the National Vietnam War Memorial Wall in Washington, D.C., this is the first memorial with the Atlanta City Seal dedicated to their sacrifice.

The project required years of fundraising and behind-the-scenes efforts by the Sons of Atlanta organization. For the park's millions of annual visitors, the memorial adds a layer of civic meaning to Atlanta's most popular public green space.
stories.imageAlt: Sons of Atlanta Vietnam War Monument and Memorial Experience in Piedmont Park

stories.headline: Construction Begins on Marquee Midtown Park at 14th Street
stories.summary: After two decades of stalled plans, site work has started on the 4-acre lot across from Four Seasons Hotel Atlanta. The interim park phase will bring a grass lawn and walking paths this summer.
stories.body:
After more than two decades of unrealized plans, construction has finally begun on a landmark greenspace in the heart of Midtown.

Midtown Alliance reports that groundwork is underway for the interim public park at 98 14th St., a 4-acre site across from Four Seasons Hotel Atlanta. This is where the lavish No2 Opus Place skyscraper and a Santiago Calatrava-designed concert hall for the Atlanta Symphony were once proposed but never built.

Field Operations, the global design firm selected after a three-month search last year, is leading the project. The firm's portfolio includes notable projects from Brooklyn to Santa Monica and China.

The first phase of temporary improvements will level and grade the site, add a grass multi-use lawn, install walking paths around the perimeter, and finally remove construction fencing that has lined 14th Street for nearly a decade. The interim park is expected to be ready for public use and community programming this summer.

For Midtown residents who have watched the empty lot sit idle through multiple failed development attempts, seeing actual construction equipment on site marks a turning point.
stories.imageAlt: Rendering of the interim park at 98 14th Street in Midtown Atlanta

stories.headline: Kirkwood's 23rd Annual Spring Fling Festival Returns May 16
stories.summary: The neighborhood's signature event returns to Bessie Branham Park with the Spring Fling Festival and Tour of Homes on Saturday, May 16.
stories.body:
Kirkwood's biggest annual event is back. The 23rd annual Spring Fling Festival and Tour of Homes returns to Bessie Branham Park on Saturday, May 16.

The festival has been a Kirkwood tradition for over two decades, drawing neighbors and visitors to one of east Atlanta's most walkable neighborhoods. The event combines a community festival atmosphere with the popular Tour of Homes, which gives attendees a look inside some of Kirkwood's most interesting houses.

For the SE BeltLine corridor, Kirkwood Spring Fling marks the unofficial start of the outdoor festival season, which runs through the fall with events across the eastside neighborhoods.
stories.imageAlt: Kirkwood Spring Fling Festival and Tour of Homes at Bessie Branham Park

stories.headline: Atlanta's Blight Tax Program Has Stalled, Audit Finds
stories.summary: An audit reveals Atlanta's program to penalize owners of blighted properties through higher taxes has not been effectively implemented, leaving problem properties in neighborhoods unaddressed.
stories.body:
Atlanta's program to combat blight through targeted tax penalties has stalled, according to a new audit.

The blight tax was designed to incentivize owners of vacant and deteriorating properties to either improve or sell them by imposing higher tax rates. But the audit found the program has not been effectively implemented, leaving problem properties across Atlanta's neighborhoods unaddressed.

For neighborhoods across the city, especially historically disinvested areas on the south and west sides, blighted properties depress surrounding home values and create safety concerns. The program was seen as a tool to force action on long-neglected lots and structures.

The audit's findings raise questions about whether the city has the administrative capacity and political will to enforce the blight tax as intended. For homeowners living next to crumbling vacant properties, the news is another frustration in a long struggle for neighborhood improvement.
stories.imageAlt: Atlanta blight tax program stalled according to audit findings

stories.headline: Atlanta-Founded Hermeus Hits $1B Valuation, Moves HQ to Los Angeles
stories.summary: The hypersonic aircraft startup raised $350M in Series C funding and is relocating its headquarters to El Segundo, CA. The Atlanta facility stays open for production with no layoffs planned.
stories.body:
Defense aviation company Hermeus hit the $1 billion unicorn valuation this week following a $350 million Series C raise ($200 million in venture capital and $150 million in debt financing), led by returning investor Khosla Ventures.

The Atlanta-founded company also announced it's moving its headquarters to El Segundo, California, directly south of LAX. Hermeus will keep its Atlanta facility open and shift its focus to production. No Atlanta layoffs are planned, and the company is actively hiring engineering talent in both cities.

Hermeus is building unmanned aircraft for national security missions, working with the Department of Defense and other government organizations. Investors include NASA, the U.S. Air Force, and Sam Altman. The team has grown to more than 275 employees across Atlanta, Los Angeles, DC, and Jacksonville.

For Atlanta's tech ecosystem, losing another HQ to California stings, but retaining the production facility means the city keeps the manufacturing jobs and technical workforce.
stories.imageAlt: Hermeus hypersonic aircraft company reaches unicorn status

stories.headline: Atlanta-Based UPS Caps Driver Buyout Program at 7,500
stories.summary: UPS is limiting its voluntary driver buyout program to 7,500 participants as the Atlanta-headquartered shipping giant works to manage costs amid shifting package volumes.
stories.body:
Atlanta-based UPS is capping its voluntary driver buyout program at 7,500 participants.

The shipping giant, headquartered in Sandy Springs, has been managing costs as package delivery volumes shift in the post-pandemic economy. The buyout program offers veteran drivers a voluntary exit package, and the cap suggests demand for the program has been significant.

For Atlanta, UPS is one of the region's largest employers, with its global headquarters and a massive logistics operation anchored at Hartsfield-Jackson International Airport. Any workforce changes at UPS ripple through the local economy.

The buyout cap comes as the broader logistics industry adjusts to changing consumer behavior, with e-commerce growth moderating from pandemic highs while business-to-business shipping patterns continue to evolve.
stories.imageAlt: UPS headquarters in Atlanta caps driver buyout program

stories.headline: Mayor Dickens Says He'll Enforce Teen Curfews After Weekend Shootings
stories.summary: Following multiple shootings over the Easter weekend, including a fatal shooting at Piedmont Park, Mayor Dickens announced plans to expand youth spaces and enforce teen curfew laws.
stories.body:
Atlanta Mayor Andre Dickens says the city will enforce teen curfew laws following a violent Easter weekend that included a fatal shooting at Piedmont Park.

The mayor's announcement came alongside plans to expand youth spaces across the city, aiming to provide alternatives for young people during evenings and weekends. The approach combines enforcement with investment in recreation and programming.

Atlanta Police are also offering a $15,000 reward for information in the Piedmont Park fatal shooting, and the department continues to search for four persons of interest in the case.

The weekend violence has prompted questions about event policies at the city's public parks and whether Atlanta is doing enough to address youth violence. For neighborhoods surrounding Piedmont Park, the shooting brought a jarring reminder that public safety challenges can affect any part of the city.
stories.imageAlt: Mayor Dickens announces teen curfew enforcement after Easter weekend violence

stories.headline: Atlanta Urology Practice to Pay $14M Settlement Over Unnecessary Procedures
stories.summary: Advanced Urology Inc. and Dr. Jitesh Patel agreed to a $14 million settlement after the U.S. Attorney's Office alleged the practice billed Medicare and Medicaid for medically unnecessary or unperformed procedures.
stories.body:
An Atlanta urology practice will pay $14 million to resolve allegations that it performed unnecessary procedures and billed for services that were never done.

The U.S. Attorney's Office for the Northern District of Georgia says Dr. Jitesh Patel and Advanced Urology Inc. agreed to the settlement to resolve claims they violated federal and state laws, including the False Claims Act.

Two whistleblowers, a former employee and a former physician, filed lawsuits raising concerns about the practice's billing and treatment patterns. Among the allegations: patients were implanted with sacral nerve stimulators without proper evaluation, numerous cystoscopy procedures were performed unnecessarily, and electromyography tests were conducted on nearly every new patient despite being rarely used in urology.

The settlement serves as a reminder for patients to ask questions about recommended procedures, especially when they seem routine or are ordered before a thorough evaluation.
stories.imageAlt: Advanced Urology practice in Atlanta agrees to $14 million settlement
